content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660               322-5901            322-6441                322-6444

February 17, 1981


Bill Cerruti
Legal Division
Department of Alcohol
and Drug Programs
111 Capitol Mall
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Mr. Cerruti:

     The purpose of this letter is to confirm in writing the
advice I gave you over the telephone. You asked about the
disclosure and disqualification obligations imposed by the
Political Reform Act on the Director of the Department of
Drug and Alcohol Programs. The facts, as I understand them,
are as follows.

     The primary function of the Department of Drug and
Alcohol Programs is to fund drug treatment programs. The
Director of this Department also sits on the board of directors
of a National Association of directors of state drug and
alcohol programs. The National Association is considering
forming a wholly owned subsidiary which would engage in
profit making activities. Specifically, the subsidiary
would carry out drug and alcohol treatment programs.

     These facts indicate that the Director may have a conflict
of interest with respect to decisions affecting the National
Association and/or the subsidiary. However, without further
information it is impossible to advise you as to whether or
not a conflict exists.

     In order to advise you, I need to have the following
information.

1.  The type of programs funded by the Department
and whether they are profit or nonprofit programs.

2.  A full description of the National Association
including its profit or nonprofit status, its sources
of funding and the character of its membership.

Bill Cerruti
February 17, 1981
Page Two


     3.  Whether and to what extent the Director is
paid or receives benefits as a result of his position
on the Board of the National Association.

     4.  A full description of the subsidiary including
its relationship to the parent and its potential relation-
ship to the Department.

The disqualification provisions of the Political Reform
Act are contained in Government Code Sections 87100-87103.
Section 87100 provides that:

     "No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest."

Since the Director may have a financial interest in the
National Association and the subsidiary, he may wish to
exercise caution by disqualifying himself from making or
influencing decisions which would have a material financial
effect on the National Association or the subsidiary.

The Director's disclosure obligations are spelled out
in the Conflict of Interest Code for the Department of Drug
and Alcohol Programs.  If you have any specific questions as
to how this code applies to the Director, please feel free
to call me.

                              Sincerely,

                              [signature]
                              Stella Connell Levy
                              Counsel
                              Legal Division

SCL:plh

sections.question_synthetic: What are the disclosure and disqualification obligations under the Political Reform Act for a Department Director who serves on the board of a national association that is considering forming a for-profit subsidiary engaged in drug and alcohol treatment programs?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Director may have a conflict of interest and should exercise caution by disqualifying himself from decisions with material financial effects on the National Association or subsidiary; however, definitive conflict analysis requires additional information about program types, funding sources, board compensation, and subsidiary details.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION section headers present; letter follows telephone advice confirmation format. Question and conclusion reconstructed from letter body. Four specific information requests outlined but no definitive conclusion provided pending receipt of additional facts.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the disclosure and disqualification obligations under the Political Reform Act for a Department Director who serves on the board of a national association that is considering forming a for-profit subsidiary engaged in drug and alcohol treatment programs?

CONCLUSION: The Director may have a conflict of interest and should exercise caution by disqualifying himself from decisions with material financial effects on the National Association or subsidiary; however, definitive conflict analysis requires additional information about program types, funding sources, board compensation, and subsidiary details.

You asked about the disclosure and disqualification obligations imposed by the Political Reform Act on the Director of the Department of Drug and Alcohol Programs. The facts, as I understand them, are as follows. The primary function of the Department of Drug and Alcohol Programs is to fund drug treatment programs. The Director of this Department also sits on the board of directors of a National Association of directors of state drug and alcohol programs. The National Association is considering forming a wholly owned subsidiary which would engage in profit making activities. Specifically, the subsidiary would carry out drug and alcohol treatment programs. These facts indicate that the Director may have a conflict of interest with respect to decisions affecting the National Association and/or the subsidiary. However, without further information it is impossible to advise you as to whether or not a conflict exists. In order to advise you, I need to have the following information. 1. The type of programs funded by the Department and whether they are profit or nonprofit programs. 2. A full description of the National Association including its profit or nonprofit status, its sources of funding and the character of its membership. 3. Whether and to what extent the Director is paid or receives benefits as a result of his position on the Board of the National Association. 4. A full description of the subsidiary including its relationship to the parent and its potential relationship to the Department. The disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act are contained in Government Code Sections 87100-87103. Section 87100 provides that: "No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." Since the Director may have a financial interest in the National Association and the subsidiary, he may wish to exercise caution by disqualifying himself from making or influencing decisions which would have a material financial effect on the National Association or the subsidiary. The Director's disclosure obligations are spelled out in the Conflict of Interest Code for the Department of Drug and Alcohol Programs. If you have any specific questions as to how this code applies to the Director, please feel free to call me. Sincerely, Stella Connell Levy Counsel Legal Division SCL:plh
embedding.summary: FPPC counsel advises a Department of Alcohol and Drug Programs Director regarding potential conflicts of interest arising from his board position with a national association considering formation of a for-profit treatment program subsidiary. The counsel identifies potential conflicts under Government Code Section 87100 but requests additional factual information before providing final advice on disqualification obligations.
